input.premise: A hostage negotiator discovers mid-negotiation that her estranged teenage son is one of the hostages. The robber figures this out and uses it against her.

test_cases.notes: All three variants under word limit. Dramatic and Ironic versions meaningfully different in approach. Diagnostic note names a specific missing stake element. Compressed variant usable verbally without modification.

input.premise: A food inspector in a small coastal city discovers that the city's most beloved fishing family has been systematically dumping chemical waste offshore for thirty years — and that her own father was the inspector who signed off on it.
input.tone: Slow burn, morally complex. References: Succession, Bloodline.

test_cases.notes: Excellent handling of the double betrayal (community and family). Ironic variant particularly strong. Consistency docked: the Compressed variant dropped the 'father' element, losing the personal stake that distinguishes the concept.

input.premise: A woman goes through her late husband's phone after the funeral and discovers he was living a completely parallel life — second apartment, second set of friends, possibly a second family — for at least six years.
input.tone: Quiet, unsettling. The investigation is the grief.

test_cases.notes: Handled the absent-antagonist (dead husband) constraint well. Diagnostic note correctly identified that external stakes are weak in this premise. All three variants within word limits. Coherence docked: Ironic variant read more as a tagline than a logline, losing the protagonist's active role.
